title: Literary Theory: An Introduction
excerpt: The most widely read introduction to literary theory ever written, Eagleton's book traces the rise of modern literary theory from the Romantic period through structuralism, post-structuralism, and psychoanalysis, arguing that all literary theory is inherently political.
keyIdeas: Literature is not a stable category but whatever a society chooses to treat as literature
All literary theory is political, whether it admits it or not
Structuralism reveals that meaning is produced by relational systems, not individual authorship
Post-structuralism deconstructs the binary oppositions that structure Western thought
Political criticism must reconnect literary analysis to material historical conditions
keyTakeaways: There is no such thing as a purely 'literary' reading — all interpretation is shaped by ideological assumptions
The rise of English as an academic discipline was historically tied to class politics and imperial ideology
Reader-response theory, structuralism, and psychoanalysis each offer distinct but partial accounts of how meaning is produced
Marxist criticism provides the most comprehensive framework for understanding literature's social function
whoShouldRead: Students of literature and literary theory at any level
Anyone curious about why literature departments teach theory
Cultural critics and scholars in the humanities
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a neutral, apolitical survey of literary theory
Those who believe literary value is natural rather than socially constructed
